The Pearl and the Pottery Shard: Rabbi Yochanan's Self-Awareness of Discovery
Reish Lakish's Praise and Agreement with the Pshat

The Source of Rabbi Akiva's Teaching: The Verse and Its Interpretation
Active Demonstration vs. Passive Prohibition: Why Lashes for Mere Existence of Kilayim

macro_segments.title: The Issue of Planting on Yom Tov: Why the Mishna Does Not List It Separately from Plowing

micro_segments.title: Ula's Challenge: Should Planting on Yom Tov Be a Separate Lashing?

Conclusion: Different Ways of Breaking Yom Tov as One Aveira

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Issur Kolel: Oaths That Take Effect on Broader Scope Than the Torah's Prohibition

macro_segments.title: Why the Mishna Does Not Mention Oaths as a Source of Additional Lashing

micro_segments.title: The Exclusion: Reversible Prohibitions Are Not Mentioned in the Mishna
Challenge: Firstborn Animals (Bechor) Are Reversible, Yet Listed
Challenge: The Nazirite (Nezir) Is Reversible, Yet Listed
Response: Nezir Shimshon Cannot Be Reversed (Angel-Appointed)
Abaye's Counter: Nezir Shimshon Is Mentioned in Hataras Nedarim—Contradiction
Resolution: The Mishna Includes Reversible Prohibitions; The Tanna Simply Doesn't Hold of Issur Kolel

macro_segments.title: The Number of Lashes: Thirty-Nine Instead of Forty, and Assessment of the Violator's Capacity

micro_segments.title: The Mishna: Lashes Are Administered as Thirty-Nine, Not Forty
The Verse: 'In the Number Forty' Is Read as Up to Forty, Not Full Forty
Rabbi Yehuda's Dissent: Full Forty Lashes, Administered Between the Shoulder Blades
Assessment of Capacity: Not All Can Withstand the Full Thirty-Nine
Lock-In Rule: Once Lashing Begins, the Number Cannot Be Changed
Rava's Assertion: The Rabbis' Authority to Reinterpret Scripture—The Supreme Court Over the Constitution
Reassessment Before vs. After Lashing Begins
Rav Sheshet's Explanation: Same-Day vs. Different-Day Assessment

macro_segments.title: One Act with Two Transgressions: Assessment and Administration of Multiple Lashings

micro_segments.title: The Mishna: One Assessment Can Bundle Two Transgressions or Treat Them Separately
Single Assessment: Limited Lashes Count Toward Both Transgressions Combined
Separate Assessments: Two Court Sessions, Each with Its Own Number

Rav Sheshet's Resolution: The Capacity Threshold of Forty-Two
Practical Effect: If Capacity Is Below Forty-Two, Only First Transgression Is Punished Today

topical_tags.term: Multiple acts and multiple transgressions from one deed

One act with two transgressions (bundled vs. separate)
